sentences.text: The only thing I know, that we went to work, and we did look forward to come home to avoid any kind of trouble.
How much you could avoid, you always run in some trouble, but you tried to escape, or whatever it is.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] That's the-- and you did look for the next day to survive.
And you have something to eat.
During the ghetto?
In a shop.

We worked for the Germans, for the public and for also military, very little, just what the Germans organized a shop for the people, the German, the way for the SS to make uniforms, all kind of stuff like this.
Right.
In that time, we had to move into the ghetto.
We used to live on [NON-ENGLISH] 5.
I think it was about eight hours, if I'm not mistaking, or more.
I don't know.
I didn't recall correctly.
Well, mostly potatoes.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] There were some people, they had a little bit more.
We got from the Germans what we worked for them.
They give us some extra bread.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] That was their own, not the-- that it belonged to us.
But some of them.
Not as far as I know.
Maybe indirectly.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] But nobody should know about this.
[PAUSES FOR 14 SECONDS]
Oh, yes.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Excuse me.
One time, they made a-- a Purim [NON-ENGLISH] I call this [NON-ENGLISH], if you know what I mean.
Yeah.
Yeah.
Yeah.
A Purim, and they took out-- they-- they come, the SS, the Gestapo.
And they said we had to bring out all the gold, silver, and money, and whatever we have.
And in-- in order to do this, they-- right away, they killed three people, that everybody would be afraid and running into the houses and bringing out whatever they have.
They killed in that time, his name was, [NON-ENGLISH] [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] [NON-ENGLISH], a young boy.
He was about 15 years old.
And then another man, he was-- he was in the ghetto, in Tomaszow.
Actually, he was from Lodz.
They were killed.
And everybody did run into the house and bring it out, like silver, whatever they could.
Oh, yeah.
Yeah, through the Jewish police and they'd run it.
This was not unusual that you could hear right away, whatever.
That was on-- on the street [NON-ENGLISH].
We had to assemble, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] and there, everybody-- whoever didn't brought it out, they said if they're going to go in into the house and find something, right away, they'll be killed.
I remember there is one [NON-ENGLISH].
They had a jewelry, a very big-- they're very rich people-- a jewelry.
And he used to come always into our house.
And he would-- some people, they had afraid even to give it.
As soon you're going to give it, they could kill you.
So what he did, he throw it on the street [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] like garbage.
And after they left, I remember we went out.
He called me because we were friend.
And we went down the street, and we find something.
He still lives in Israel, in Haifa.
I just talked to him last year about this, that incident.
You had spoken, or you had said at the beginning of this [NON-ENGLISH] or at the-- after the Jews had been assembled, three Jews were shot.
Did you witness their shooting?
Yeah, right near me.
Samuelson was killed right near me.
He shot-- his name was Kemp, a Gestapo.
He just took him-- oh, dear.
He just, with the pistol, he-- here, mostly, they shot here, right here.
And he did roll over.
No.
I could be picked just the same as anybody else.
Right.
I, I remember we brought the candlelight, the silver, and some other things, [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] whatever we could.
Sometime, you forgot to-- to bring something out too.
No, as fast as possible.
Yeah.
Everybody did panic.
It's unbelievable panic.
Everybody did run, and it didn't take minutes.
Everybody came back.
[PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] But they didn't go later on into the houses.
[PAUSES FOR 9 SECONDS]
Right.
It's even the Jewish police, some were very good and some were bad--
--unfortunately.
Kapos, Jewish kapos.
Unfortunately, that was true.
There was-- there was one guy in our place.
Even he was a neighbor of us.
His name was [NON-ENGLISH].
He was a terrible man.
He said, should one hair from my head fall out, I rather see 10 or 100 Jews killed.
He was not bad to me, personally.
It's a matter of fact, he wanted to make me as a Jewish policeman.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] But I'm not the type.
It's not me.
And he was very much in contact with that [NON-ENGLISH], like friend.
A Jewish policeman.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] He was a terrible man.
When we were in the first camp in Blizyn, we did work in the shop.
They give us thread in there.
And everybody had to look after this like, I don't know, not to get lost.
And, you know, they didn't give the threads.
Make it.
We weren't going to make it.
Everybody, they try to keep it so that it doesn't get lost.
I remember a boy from 16 years, he was from Bialystok.
And he forgot to put it out that-- that thread.
And he had a small spool of thread.
And he had it in his pocket.
And after the shift, we went out, and that same [NON-ENGLISH], he noticed that spool of this.
He went over to the shop fuehrer, the German.
His name was Meyerlink, a German.
And he said, and they give him a gift.
That boy, over next day, for this, he got killed, that boy, a 16-year-old boy.
He was the same thing, but he was like a, let's say, he collaborated with the Germans, in one word.
Everybody had to-- had to do it for themself.
That did take place some things, you know, how to escape, or whether there is some people.
But some people did escape, you know, from the ghetto, and lived outside the ghetto.
Later on, when that happened, we got to know.
But everybody was so organized secretly, because you never knew what the other person is doing
The liquidation was November-- you mean when they liquidate everything?
November the 30th.
And it come out on a Saturday.
They liquidated half of the people.
In 1942, that was.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Yeah.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] They-- that's right, November the 30th, 1942.
And they send it to we didn't know where.
And then Sunday-- it was Sunday.
That was on a Saturday.
Yeah.
There were not any notice, as far as I know.
And they just came in, and they took part of Tomaszow, where the ghetto is.
Like the ghetto were not so big, like in Lodz or in Warsaw.
But part of the streets where the ghetto is, that-- that part goes out on Saturday.
And then-- which I didn't see it, because you couldn't go.
It was like a spare.
You couldn't go on the street.
And they took one part.
And then the first-- in the 2nd of November was the second part where they took it out.
This, I remember correctly, the second part, because we always make a memorial day the 2nd of November.
And that was liquidate.
And there was left only the people-- the workers like me and others in a separate place.
Yeah, sure, the Ukraines, the Latvians, the Latvians.
They were involved to a certain they-- let's say they-- some Jewish policemen, they went to the train station with the people, what they thought they going to be spared.
On the station, they had to go in in the-- in the cars too, the police, the Jewish police.
Right.
That's what I was told.
And, it's a matter of fact, we didn't see, later on, those policemen.
So that means they went.
All-- we had cards.
And the cards were stamped from the Gestapo, like we are working for-- whoever did work for the Gestapo, for the Germans.
And I had a card like this too.
And I went with my parents and with my sister, my youngest sister, to our little church, where they were assembled.
And there they'd stay-- the Gestapo, only the Gestapo that stayed there, with page-- with the pages, yeah?
What you-- how do you call this?
The-- you know, when they want to-- like, they're the-- With weapons?
Weapons.
Clubs.
That's right.
Let it stay there, and they did look over the cards.
So whoever had a stamp, they sent it into the church.
Whoever didn't-- my father, my mother didn't have.
I had.
My sister had.
And they sended me over.
In that time, they-- my parents, they sended another-- another way, which they, later on, they went to the train and go to Treblinka.
That was the end of it.
Yeah.
With my parents, from the house where we used to live.
Oh, a lot of people.
My family members, they were before.
They were before in the church.
Not in the church, on the lot from the church.
And some of them, they went already.
It's a matter of fact, when we came into the church lot, I saw my brother-in-law.
And then he said that he wanted to go with his wife.
So they said, OK.
They took him out.
And they sent him to-- with his wife.
Yeah, because he had the stamp.
He was already in the churchyard.
So that means that he was authorized.
No.
The whole--
No, Haskell was in Russia.
Yeah.
And I did walk, my brother, Nojech, and his wife, and his daughter, the six-year-old daughter.
And they were taken away.
And we had to go into the yard.
No.
No.
The only thing that I know, my sister, while she is in Toronto, she didn't want to leave Mother.
And when you come near the Gestapo, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] they just clubbed you.
You had to go here, and you had to go here.
Yeah.
No.
Nothing, just standing.
Yeah, you just didn't know what to do.
Whatever it should be, it should be over in minutes.
We were so afraid just looking at them with the dogs that stay in-- [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] oh, dear, it wasn't--
When we walked there to the church.
That was the last.
[PAUSES FOR 12 SECONDS]
Yeah, till we went to Blizyn.
No.
We went into the churchyard, and they went to go farther.
Right away, they went right to the station.
We didn't know anything.
We didn't understand anything.
Later on, we found out.
[PAUSES FOR 8 SECONDS]
The next day, we had to go to work.
Yeah, in the shop.
All of them.
Maybe about, I would-- I don't think so even 500 people were left.
Within that time-- in that time, you know, before the war, was about 15,000 Jews.
And later on, during the war, a lot of people did come from all of over-- from Lodz, from Lezne, from all over, they did come to Tomaszow.
I would say it was about, for sure about double, 30,000 or more.
I don't know exactly, because everybody, they thought, here is better.
In Tomaszow, will be better, which it was very bad  in Lodz.
We had a lot of people from Lodz, Jewish people, a lot.
Till we went to Blizyn.
I marked it down, but I have it on the table, this.
That was in 1944 or '43.
We went to Blizyn.
And that was in June-- I marked it down there.
By train, by, you know, those-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] you know, the-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] like what they are-- no, where they are transporting cows and pigs and--
Cattle cars.
Closed.
Yeah.
Well, maybe some people did react more than I did.
I didn't know anything about-- we went in.
If we had to go, we would have to go.
That's all.
That's what I said.
No, no, no, just with clubs and just get out and on and this.
That wasn't smooth, not at all.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] It was just the beating and beating, and that's all.
We didn't know where we are going, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] till we did arrive there in Blizyn.
Oh, it was very bad-- very, very bad, because there were a lot of people in one car.
And the sanitary, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] unbelievable.
You know, I personally had a need to go to the washroom and, yet, very bad.
Not at all.
Nothing was provided.
I think it was about five or six hours, something like this.
It's not very far, but it took us a long time.
The-- also the Ukrainians and Germans.
Yeah, with my brother.
My older brother, Nojech.
They were deported, and they were sent to Treblinka before.
No.
No.
Some in the same and some two days before.
Like my sister Cluwa was transport-- was transported the 30th of November.
And also--
Yeah, yeah.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Oh, to Treblinka.
And so the-- and we, after-- in 1944, we were transported to Blizyn.
When that was [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] 1943, '42, yeah, 1942, I've heard say.
Everything gets mixed.
OK.
